name: KaRaMeL / Low* — F* Pipeline to Verified C
summary: KaRaMeL (formerly KreMLin) extracts a verified C-extractable subset of F* called Low* into readable C; Low* programs verify against a stack/heap memory model, integrated with F*'s effect system (ICFP 2017). The pipeline underlies Project Everest's HACL*, EverCrypt, EverParse, and miTLS; HACL*/EverCrypt code ships in Mozilla Firefox NSS, the Linux kernel WireGuard implementation, mbedTLS, Microsoft WinQuic, the Tezos blockchain, and the ElectionGuard SDK.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Dependently typed ML with effects (F\*) plus a verified C-extractable subset (Low\*); Low\* programs verify against a stack/heap memory model and compile to readable C via **KaRaMeL** (formerly KreMLin). The F\* / Low\* / KaRaMeL pipeline is also catalogued in `data/verification-intermediate-languages` as part of the verification-IL family alongside Boogie, Viper, and WhyML.

**Low\* memory model** (ICFP 2017) — region-based stack/heap with abstract pointers, integrated with F\*'s effect system. **EverCrypt agile multiplexer**: single verified API selects HACL\* (C) or ValeCrypt (asm) at runtime, sealing the abstraction. **Vale/F\* integration** connects C-level and assembly-level proofs against a shared spec.

This record covers the **KaRaMeL transpiler and the Low\* subset** specifically; the full F\* language including the refinement-type/effect system is covered in `data/fstar`.

sections.title: Low* — verified C-extractable subset
sections.content:
**Low\*** is the C-extractable subset of F\* (ICFP 2017). It restricts F\*'s feature set to constructs that map directly to C: scalar types, fixed-width integers, mutable references, arrays of fixed length, structs, and pointer arithmetic within bounded regions.

The **memory model** is region-based: each function operates inside a stack frame (a region scoped to the call), with pointers carrying explicit region annotations. The F\* effect system tracks which regions a function may read and write, enabling the verifier to discharge memory-safety obligations (no use-after-free, no out-of-bounds, no aliasing violations) without runtime checks. This is unusual among C verifiers — most use a flat heap model and require manual disjointness reasoning, where Low\*'s region discipline makes the disjointness automatic from the type signatures.

The key insight: **a verification-focused IL paired with a code-extractable subset gives both proof and production binary from one source**. Low\* programs simultaneously serve as the proof artefact (verified in F\*) and the source of the production C binary (extracted by KaRaMeL).

sections.title: KaRaMeL — Low* to readable C
sections.content:
**KaRaMeL** (formerly KreMLin) extracts Low\* programs to **readable C**. The output is not just "any C" but C that engineers without F\* expertise can audit: meaningful identifier names, compatible struct layouts, idiomatic control flow. This was an explicit design goal — verified-cryptography deployments need C code that downstream consumers (Mozilla, the Linux kernel) will accept as part of their codebase, not opaque generated artefacts.

KaRaMeL is part of the trusted base: a bug in KaRaMeL could produce miscompiled C from a verified Low\* source. In practice the extraction is conservative (close to a structural transcription rather than an optimising compiler) which keeps the trusted code path narrow.
